content.text: Deploying GenAI features in an enterprise context introduces new consent obligations that go beyond standard data processing. When a user's data is used to retrieve context for a language model, fine-tune a model, or personalise responses, each of those uses is a separate processing purpose under GDPR Article 6 and similar frameworks — and each may require its own consent signal. This sub-subtopic covers how to design a consent capture system that is granular, auditable, and propagated to all downstream consumers.

attrs.alt: Consent capture flow for GenAI features

content.text: Unlike a simple cookie banner, GenAI consent must distinguish between fundamentally different data uses. Bundling them into a single consent checkbox fails the GDPR requirement for freely given, specific, informed consent.
Using the user's data as retrieval context in RAG queries
Including user interactions in model fine-tuning datasets
"Help improve the AI with my conversations"
Recording interaction telemetry for quality monitoring
Storing a user preference profile to tailor responses
Sharing retrieved context or preferences across applications
Explicit consent required (Art. 22 if automated decisions)
Users must not be enrolled in training data collection by default. Pre-ticked checkboxes and opt-out-only designs are invalid under GDPR Recital 32.

from __future__ import annotations
import enum
import uuid
import hashlib
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

class ConsentPurpose(enum.Enum):
    RAG_RETRIEVAL       = "rag_retrieval"
    FINE_TUNE_TRAINING  = "fine_tune_training"
    USAGE_ANALYTICS     = "usage_analytics"
    PERSONALISATION     = "personalisation"
    CROSS_APP_SHARING   = "cross_app_sharing"
    PROFILING           = "profiling"

class ConsentMechanism(enum.Enum):
    EXPLICIT_CHECKBOX   = "explicit_checkbox"    # user ticked a checkbox
    SETTINGS_TOGGLE     = "settings_toggle"      # user flipped a toggle in settings
    API_CALL            = "api_call"             # service-to-service (admin consent)
    IMPLICIT_TERMS      = "implicit_terms"       # embedded in accepted ToS (must be specific section)

@dataclass
class ConsentRecord:
    record_id: str
    subject_id: str                # user ID or organisation ID
    purpose: ConsentPurpose
    granted: bool                  # True = granted; False = withdrawn
    mechanism: ConsentMechanism
    policy_version: str            # version of the privacy policy at time of consent
    app_id: str                    # which application captured this
    captured_at: datetime
    ip_hash: Optional[str]         # SHA-256 of IP address for audit (not raw IP)
    user_agent_hash: Optional[str]
    expires_at: Optional[datetime] # None = indefinite (re-verify on policy change)
    withdrawn_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    withdrawal_reason: Optional[str] = None

    @classmethod
    def grant(
        cls,
        subject_id: str,
        purpose: ConsentPurpose,
        mechanism: ConsentMechanism,
        policy_version: str,
        app_id: str,
        ip: Optional[str] = None,
        user_agent: Optional[str] = None,
        expires_at: Optional[datetime] = None,
    ) -> "ConsentRecord":
        def _hash(val: Optional[str]) -> Optional[str]:
            return hashlib.sha256(val.encode()).hexdigest() if val else None

        return cls(
            record_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
            subject_id=subject_id,
            purpose=purpose,
            granted=True,
            mechanism=mechanism,
            policy_version=policy_version,
            app_id=app_id,
            captured_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc),
            ip_hash=_hash(ip),
            user_agent_hash=_hash(user_agent),
            expires_at=expires_at,
        )

    def is_active(self) -> bool:
        """True if granted and not withdrawn and not expired."""
        if not self.granted or self.withdrawn_at is not None:
            return False
        if self.expires_at and datetime.now(timezone.utc) > self.expires_at:
            return False
        return True
Consent records must be immutable once written. Withdrawals do not delete the original grant — they add a new record with
. This preserves a complete audit trail for regulators: when consent was given, under which policy version, and when it was withdrawn.

The consent API exposes endpoints that the UI and service-to-service callers use. It validates the policy version, records the choice, and publishes a domain event to the event bus so downstream systems can update their gates synchronously.

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

CURRENT_POLICY_VERSION = "2024-Q1"

@dataclass
class ConsentUpdateRequest:
    subject_id: str
    purpose: str             # ConsentPurpose value string
    granted: bool
    mechanism: str           # ConsentMechanism value string
    app_id: str
    ip: str
    user_agent: str

@dataclass
class ConsentUpdatedEvent:
    subject_id: str
    purpose: str
    granted: bool
    policy_version: str
    event_type: str          # "consent.granted" or "consent.withdrawn"

class ConsentService:
    def __init__(
        self,
        store: ConsentStore,
        publish_event: Callable[[ConsentUpdatedEvent], None],
    ) -> None:
        self._store = store
        self._publish = publish_event

    def update_consent(self, req: ConsentUpdateRequest) -> ConsentRecord:
        purpose = ConsentPurpose(req.purpose)
        mechanism = ConsentMechanism(req.mechanism)

        if req.granted:
            record = ConsentRecord.grant(
                subject_id=req.subject_id,
                purpose=purpose,
                mechanism=mechanism,
                policy_version=CURRENT_POLICY_VERSION,
                app_id=req.app_id,
                ip=req.ip,
                user_agent=req.user_agent,
            )
            self._store.record_grant(record)
            event_type = "consent.granted"
        else:
            record = self._store.record_withdrawal(req.subject_id, purpose)
            if record is None:
                raise ValueError(f"No existing consent for {req.subject_id}/{req.purpose}")
            event_type = "consent.withdrawn"

        self._publish(ConsentUpdatedEvent(
            subject_id=req.subject_id,
            purpose=req.purpose,
            granted=req.granted,
            policy_version=CURRENT_POLICY_VERSION,
            event_type=event_type,
        ))
        return record

    def check_consent(self, subject_id: str, purpose: ConsentPurpose) -> bool:
        return self._store.has_active_consent(subject_id, purpose)
Enforcing Consent in the RAG Retriever
The retriever is the enforcement point for

consent. Before issuing any query, it checks whether the requesting user has an active grant for that purpose. Without it, the retriever either returns an empty result set or raises an exception that the application layer converts into a user-facing explanation.

When the privacy policy changes in a way that materially affects how data is processed, existing consents tied to the old policy version become invalid and must be re-captured. The system must track which policy version each consent was given under and flag users who need to re-consent.
Capture fresh explicit consent for new purpose
Banner or settings prompt on next login
Notify only; no new consent needed
Depends on scope; re-consent if materially new
Users who consented to affected purposes
Re-capture consent (scope change is a new purpose)
Users with existing consent for that purpose
No re-consent needed; log policy version bump
must be enforced in the data pipeline — not just in the retriever. Every training dataset export and analytics event must be filtered through the consent store before inclusion.
Each purpose must have its own checkbox. A single "I agree" bundling all purposes is invalid.
Every opt-in starts unticked. For optional purposes (analytics, personalisation), the default state must be unchecked.
The purpose name shown to the user must describe the actual use in non-legal terms. "Help improve the AI" is acceptable; "Processing under Article 6(1)(f)" is not sufficient on its own.
Users must be able to withdraw any individual consent at any time through the same settings panel where they granted it. The process must not be harder than granting.
When a user withdraws consent, display what will change: "Your conversations will no longer be used to train the AI. Previously collected data will be excluded from the next training run."

Consent capture for GenAI features requires purpose-level granularity, an immutable append-only record for each grant and withdrawal, and enforcement gates at every consumer — the retriever, the fine-tuning pipeline, analytics, and personalisation. Policy version tracking ensures that when processing purposes change, affected users are identified and prompted to re-consent before their data continues to be used.
